methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $350M last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 5 same-sector + same-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $300M - $10.0B.

private.name: 1X Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $10B

private.name: Bedrock Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.75B

private.name: Dexterity
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.65B

private.name: Collaborative Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $500M+

private.name: GrayMatter Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $300M+

data.narrative:
Fairmat is a Series B robotics and manufacturing company founded by Benjamin Saada, currently valued at $350M post-money following ~$103M raised to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comp universe of nine private robotics firms at similar stage, supplemented by one public comparable (277810.KQ).

Two triangulation methods are applicable. Stage-progression modeling applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B-to-next-stage advancement, yielding $665M. Peer-median benchmarking across five same-sector, same-stage comparables—1X Technologies ($10.0B), Bedrock Robotics ($1.8B), Dexterity ($1.6B), Collaborative Robotics ($500M), and GrayMatter Robotics ($300M)—produces a median implied value of $1.6B, with a range spanning $300M to $10.0B. The wide peer dispersion reflects heterogeneous technology risk and commercialization progress within the robotics sector.

Triangulating both methods yields a valuation range of $565M (low) to $1.9B (high), with a median of $1.2B. The current $350M valuation sits substantially below the low-end estimate, suggesting either material downside risk relative to peer cohort or execution risk not fully reflected in comparable valuations. Key uncertainties include the applicability of the 1.9x stage multiplier to frontier robotics ventures, the representativeness of the peer set given extreme valuation dispersion, and the extent to which Fairmat's technology differentiation or market positioning justifies valuation within or beyond the estimated range.
